assistant_message.content:
{
  "finding_id": "openscap_026",
  "rule_id": "Disable GDM Automatic Login",
  "category": "safe_to_remediate",
  "confidence": 0.96,
  "rationale": "The remediation only edits /etc/gdm/custom.conf to disable automatic login, a standard STIG hardening step. It does not remove essential services or credentials and poses minimal risk of lockout. GDM will simply require user authentication on the next graphical login.",
  "risk_factors": [
    "Potential mis‑syntax in custom.conf could prevent GDM from starting",
    "If the file does not exist, the script creates/updates it which may affect custom configurations"
  ],
  "safe_next_steps": [
    "Backup /etc/gdm/custom.conf before applying the change",
    "Verify the file syntax after editing",
    "Restart the gdm service (systemctl restart gdm) or log out/in to apply the change",
    "Check that graphical login prompts for credentials"
  ],
  "requires_reboot": false,
  "touches_authn_authz": true,
  "touches_networking": false,
  "touches_filesystems": false
}
assistant_message.reasoning:
We need to classify the finding: Disable GDM Automatic Login. This modifies /etc/gdm/custom.conf to set AutomaticLoginEnable=False. This is an auth/authz related change (login). It's unlikely to cause lockout. Changing GDM may affect graphical login but not lock out console. So safe_to_remediate.

Set confidence high, maybe 0.97.

Provide rationale, risk_factors, safe_next_steps, requires_reboot maybe false (changing config may require GDM restart, but not reboot). So false. touches_authn_authz true. touches_networking false. touches_filesystems false.

Return JSON only.
